programs.name: MI Health Link
programs.tagline: Combines your parent's Medicare and Medicaid into one coordinated care plan with no co-pays

localEntities.name: Regional ICOs varying by eligible counties

programs.intro:
If your parent has both Medicare and Medicaid in select Michigan counties, MI Health Link combines their care into one coordinated program. Because all medical care, medications, and care coordination go through one team, your parent won't juggle multiple providers or pay co-pays and deductibles for in-network services.

Your parent must live in Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, Macomb, St. Joseph, Van Buren, Wayne counties, or any Upper Peninsula county. They need full Medicaid eligibility — not Medicaid with spend down — and cannot be enrolled in PACE or MI Choice programs.

summary: Not in PACE or MI Choice
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For home care services, your parent must need daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar tasks (assessed through Michigan's clinical evaluation)
otherRequirements: Must have both Medicare and full Medicaid
Cannot be in hospice care
Cannot live in state-operated veteran's home
Must leave PACE or MI Choice if currently enrolled

applicationGuide.summary: Eligible individuals receive enrollment letters automatically, or contact your local Medicaid office.

steps.title: Check if you received an enrollment letter
steps.description: Michigan ENROLLS automatically sends letters to eligible individuals

steps.title: Complete consent forms
steps.description: Fill out Authorization to Disclose Protected Health Information (DCH-1183) and Behavioral Health Consent Form (MDHHS-5515)

steps.title: Provide documentation
steps.description: Submit proof of Medicare and Medicaid eligibility, county residency, and medical records if seeking home care services

steps.title: Choose your regional care organization
steps.description: Select from available Integrated Care Organizations in your county

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is currently in PACE or MI Choice, they must leave those programs to join MI Health Link — contact both programs to coordinate the transition.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance with face value under $1,500

categories.name: Insurance
items: Current Medicare Part D plan information

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records if seeking home care services
Assessment documentation for nursing facility level of care

contentSections.text: MI Health Link replaces your parent's Medicare Part D drug plan — they cannot keep both. Make sure their current medications are covered before switching.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors?
faqs.answer: Only if their doctors are in the MI Health Link network for your county's Integrated Care Organization. Your parent will need to switch to in-network providers to avoid co-pays and get coordinated care.

faqs.question: What happens to Mom's Medicare Part D drug plan?
faqs.answer: MI Health Link replaces Medicare Part D completely. Your mom cannot keep both. Check that her current medications are covered under MI Health Link before enrolling.

faqs.question: Can Dad get paid family caregiving through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your dad qualifies for home and community-based services and meets Nursing Facility Level of Care, the program can pay family members to provide personal care services based on a needs assessment.

faqs.question: What if we live just outside the eligible counties?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot enroll in MI Health Link. The program only serves Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, Macomb, St. Joseph, Van Buren, Wayne counties, and Upper Peninsula counties.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both MI Choice and MI Health Link?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must leave MI Choice or PACE programs to join MI Health Link. You'll need to coordinate the transition between programs to avoid gaps in care.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for regular Medicaid but they have high medical bills?
faqs.answer: If your parent has Medicaid with spend down (pays a monthly amount toward medical bills), they cannot join MI Health Link. The program requires full Medicaid eligibility without spend down.

programs.name: MI Choice Medicaid Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily care help but wants to stay home, this waiver pays for home health, adult day care, and other nursing home-level services at home.

localEntities.name: Regional AAAs and partners (e.g., AAANM, CareWell Services, The Senior Alliance, Area Agency on Aging)

programs.intro:
If your aging parent needs help with bathing, dressing, or managing medications but wants to avoid a nursing home, MI Choice may pay for home health aides, adult day programs, meal delivery, and other services that let them stay home safely. This Medicaid waiver covers the same level of care they'd get in a nursing facility — but delivered at home or in the community.

Your parent must be 65+ (or disabled if younger) and meet nursing home-level care requirements through a clinical assessment. They also need to qualify financially: monthly income under $2,901 and countable assets under $9,950 (home and one car don't count). The program is available statewide but administered through regional agencies, so wait times and specific services may vary by area.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or has cognitive impairment requiring supervision)
otherRequirements: Must be Medicaid eligible
Must require supports coordination plus at least one other waiver service
Cannot currently live in a nursing facility

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional MI Choice agency for a phone screening, followed by an in-home assessment if you qualify.

steps.title: Call your regional MI Choice agency
steps.description: Contact your area's agency for initial phone screening to determine if your parent meets basic eligibility requirements.

steps.title: Complete phone screening
steps.description: Agency staff will ask about your parent's income, assets, medical conditions, and daily care needs over the phone.

steps.title: Schedule in-home assessment
steps.description: If eligible, a nurse or social worker will visit your parent's home to conduct the Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment.

steps.title: Develop care plan
steps.description: If approved, the supports coordinator will create a personalized service plan based on your parent's specific needs and preferences.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Initial phone screening followed by in-home assessment if qualified; timeline varies by region
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible if program at capacity in your region; placement after screening
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's medical records and recent bank statements ready before calling — the phone screening covers both health and financial eligibility.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Second vehicles beyond the first car
Real estate other than primary residence
excluded: Primary residence (home where your parent lives)
Burial funds up to allowable limits

categories.name: Financial
items: Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records or physician notes

offices.name: Area Agency on Aging of Northwest Michigan (AAANM)
offices.address: Serves northern counties including Antrim and Grand Traverse

contentSections.text: Assets transferred or sold below fair market value within the past 60 months trigger penalty periods that delay eligibility. Your parent's home is protected while they receive benefits, but Michigan can recover costs from the estate after death.

A dementia diagnosis alone doesn't guarantee approval — your parent must still pass the functional assessment showing they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she gets MI Choice?
faqs.answer: Yes, her primary residence doesn't count toward the $9,950 asset limit and she can continue living there. However, Michigan may recover program costs from her estate after death through Medicaid Estate Recovery.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is $3,000 per month — just over the limit?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify. The income limit is firm at $2,901 per month in 2025. However, if he's married, each spouse is assessed individually — so if Mom has lower income, she might still qualify even if Dad doesn't.

faqs.question: Can family members get paid to provide care through MI Choice?
faqs.answer: Yes, MI Choice can pay family caregivers for personal care services. This is arranged through the individualized care plan and managed through fiscal intermediary services.

faqs.question: How is this different from regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Regular Medicaid covers medical care but limited home services. MI Choice adds comprehensive home and community services like personal care, adult day programs, home modifications, and care coordination — services that would otherwise require nursing home placement.

faqs.question: What happens if there's a waitlist in our area?
faqs.answer: You'll be placed on the waiting list after the screening process. Priority is typically given based on urgency of need and assessment scores. Wait times vary significantly by region — northern Michigan may have shorter waits than metro Detroit.

faqs.question: Does Mom need to be on Medicaid already to apply?
faqs.answer: No, but she must be financially eligible for Medicaid. The MI Choice application process includes Medicaid eligibility determination, so you can apply for both programs simultaneously.

programs.name: Michigan Program for All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care, transportation, and daily support under one team.

localEntities.name: Ascension Living PACE Michigan
PACE of Southwest Michigan (Berrien, Cass, Van Buren Counties)
PACE Southeast Michigan
PACE Northeast Michigan (Alcona, Alpena, Presque Isle, Montmorency Counties; parts of Iosco)
Senior Care Partners P.A.C.E.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, PACE provides all their medical care, transportation, meals, and home support under one coordinated team. Because PACE covers everything from doctor visits to prescription drugs to adult day programs, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers — one interdisciplinary team manages their complete care plan.

PACE is only available in specific areas of Michigan through five regional providers. Your parent must qualify for nursing home level care but be able to live safely at home with PACE support. If they have both Medicare and Medicaid, all services are free with no copays or deductibles. If they only have Medicare, they pay a monthly premium based on their income and assets.

summary: Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by the state as needing nursing home level care (daily assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in approved PACE provider service area
Able to live safely in community with PACE support
Cannot be enrolled in MIChoice waiver or HMO

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local PACE provider directly for an intake assessment — they handle everything including Medicaid applications.

steps.title: Find your PACE provider
steps.description: Check if your parent lives in a PACE service area using the provider directory below. Each area has one exclusive provider.

steps.title: Call for intake assessment
steps.description: Contact your PACE provider by phone or website. They'll schedule an in-person assessment at their center.

steps.title: Complete enrollment assessment
steps.description: The interdisciplinary team evaluates if your parent meets nursing home level care and can live safely in the community with PACE support.

steps.title: PACE handles Medicaid application
steps.description: If your parent needs Medicaid for no-cost coverage, PACE assists with the application and gathers required financial documents.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by provider after intake and enrollment assessments
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible — varies by region and provider
applicationGuide.tip: PACE providers help determine if your parent qualifies for Medicaid to avoid monthly premiums, so don't worry about calculating income limits yourself.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits for Free Coverage
excluded: Burial funds up to reasonable amounts

offices.name: PACE Southeast Michigan
offices.address: Designated zip codes in Southeast Michigan

offices.name: PACE Northeast Michigan
offices.address: Alcona, Alpena, Presque Isle, Montmorency Counties; parts of Iosco County

offices.name: Senior Care Partners P.A.C.E.
offices.address: Mapped service area — check with provider

categories.name: Medical Records
items: Recent medical records or physician referrals

categories.name: Financial (if applying for Medicaid)
items: Recent pay stubs or pension statements

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide. You must live in the specific service area of one of five approved providers. Use each provider's website to verify your parent's address qualifies.
Your parent must receive ALL medical care through PACE except emergencies. Going to outside providers without authorization may result in charges to your parent.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your mom's primary home doesn't count toward asset limits, regardless of value. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes instead of moving to nursing facilities.

faqs.question: What if Dad has Medicare but makes too much for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: He can still enroll in PACE and pay a monthly premium based on his income and assets. The PACE provider will calculate the premium amount and explain costs during the assessment.

faqs.question: Can Mom still see her current doctor?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires your mom to receive all medical care through their interdisciplinary team, except for emergency situations. This coordination is how PACE manages comprehensive care.

faqs.question: What if we live on the border between two PACE service areas?
faqs.answer: Each PACE provider has exclusive service areas designated by the state. Use your exact address to check eligibility on each provider's website, as neighboring areas may be served by different organizations.

faqs.question: How is this different from MIChoice waiver?
faqs.answer: PACE provides all medical care plus home support, while MIChoice only covers home care services. Your parent cannot be enrolled in both programs simultaneously — you must choose one.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad needs nursing home care later?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care when medically necessary. The same interdisciplinary team continues coordinating his care, so there's no disruption in coverage or provider relationships.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) - QMB, SLMB, QI
programs.tagline: Get help paying Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays if your parent's income is under $1,585/month.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs, Michigan's Medicare Savings Programs can help. These three programs — QMB, SLMB, and QI — pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays based on your parent's income level. QMB provides the most coverage (pays all Medicare costs) for those with income under $1,350/month. SLMB pays the $202.90/month Medicare Part B premium for income up to $1,585/month. QI also pays Part B premiums plus provides prescription drug help, but operates on first-come, first-served basis.

Each program has different rules about when coverage starts. QMB only covers costs going forward from approval — no retroactive help. SLMB can go back up to 3 months if your parent was eligible then. QI coverage starts from January of the approval year, even if approved later. The key requirement: your parent must already have Medicare.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premium ($202.90/month) plus deductibles and copays

otherRequirements: Must have Medicare Part A and/or Part B
SLMB and QI require premium-free Medicare Part A
QI applicants cannot have other Medicaid coverage
QI requires annual reapplication

applicationGuide.summary: Call Michigan's Medicaid line at 800-642-3195 — approval takes up to 45 days.

steps.title: Gather Medicare and income documents
steps.description: You'll need Medicare card, Social Security statements, pension records, and bank statements.

steps.title: Call MDHHS at 800-642-3195
steps.description: Or contact MI Options or SHIP for application guidance.

steps.title: Submit income and asset verification
steps.description: Provide all requested documentation to complete the application.

steps.title: Wait for approval decision
steps.description: QMB decisions take up to 45 days. Coverage starts the month after approval.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days for QMB; timeline not specified for SLMB and QI
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program operates first-come, first-served with limited funding
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI early in the year — funding runs out and it's first-come, first-served.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Program Type (2026)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits, effective April 1. Income limits change annually.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Resources and assets (specific items not detailed by state)
excluded: Asset exemptions vary by state — contact MDHHS for complete list

contentSections.text: QI program funding is limited and operates first-come, first-served. Previous QI recipients get priority, but apply early in the year to avoid missing out.
QMB coverage only goes forward from approval — it won't pay Medicare costs from before your approval date. SLMB can go back up to 3 months if you were eligible then.

faqs.question: What's the difference between QMB, SLMB, and QI?
faqs.answer: QMB pays all Medicare costs (premiums, deductibles, copays) for income under $1,350/month. SLMB only pays the $202.90/month Part B premium for income up to $1,585/month. QI pays Part B premium plus provides prescription drug help, but has limited funding and requires yearly reapplication.

faqs.question: Will this program pay for Medicare costs from previous months?
faqs.answer: It depends which program. QMB only covers costs going forward from approval — no retroactive coverage. SLMB can cover up to 3 months back if your parent was income-eligible during those months. QI coverage starts from January of the approval year.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Asset limits are $9,950 for individuals and $14,910 for couples, but Michigan doesn't specify which assets count toward this limit. Contact MDHHS at 800-642-3195 to understand what assets are exempt, including home equity rules.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits are firm: $1,350 for QMB, $1,585 for SLMB (single person). However, limits increase every April 1st. If he's close, consider applying when the new limits take effect or explore if any income can be excluded.

faqs.question: Does Mom need to reapply every year?
faqs.answer: Only for QI — it requires annual reapplication and operates first-come, first-served. QMB and SLMB don't require yearly reapplication, but the state will review eligibility periodically.

faqs.question: Can Dad have this program and regular Medicaid at the same time?
faqs.answer: QMB and SLMB can work alongside other programs. But QI specifically excludes anyone who qualifies for other Medicaid coverage. If your dad has SSI-related Medicaid, he automatically gets QMB without a separate application.

programs.name: SNAP Food Assistance (MiCAFE for seniors)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 60+ on a fixed income, they may qualify for $23–292/month toward groceries with simplified application help.

localEntities.name: MiCAFE Network partners via Elder Law of Michigan; statewide access via 1-877-664-2233.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling to afford groceries on a fixed income, MiCAFE helps them apply for SNAP food benefits with personalized assistance. Because nearly 50% of eligible seniors don't apply due to stigma or confusion, MiCAFE provides dedicated phone support and even in-home application help to overcome these barriers.

Your parent receives a Michigan Bridge Card that works like a debit card for groceries at supermarkets, farmers markets, and most food stores. Single seniors typically receive $23–292/month depending on income and expenses, with higher income limits (200% of Federal Poverty Level) specifically for seniors compared to younger households.

Because seniors 60+ get special deductions for medical expenses and shelter costs, your parent may qualify even if their income seems too high at first glance. MiCAFE specialists help calculate these deductions to maximize benefits and handle annual recertification.
programs.savingsSource: Based on monthly benefit range of $23–292 for single seniors

summary: Gross income below 200% Federal Poverty Level
otherRequirements: Must live in Michigan
U.S. citizen or acceptable immigrant status
Net monthly income at or below 100% Federal Poverty Level after deductions

applicationGuide.summary: Call MiCAFE at 1-877-664-2233 for personalized application help, or apply online at michigan.gov/mibridges.

steps.title: Call MiCAFE specialists
steps.description: Call 1-877-664-2233 (Monday-Friday, 9:00 AM - 3:00 PM) for application assistance tailored to seniors. They can schedule in-home help if needed.

steps.title: Gather income and expense documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security statements, any other income, rent/mortgage statements, utility bills, and medical expense receipts (medical expenses count as deductions for seniors).

steps.title: Complete application with guidance
steps.description: MiCAFE staff help you apply online through MI Bridges or complete forms by phone, ensuring you claim all senior-specific deductions.

steps.title: Receive Bridge Card
steps.description: Once approved, your parent gets a Michigan Bridge Card loaded monthly with SNAP benefits for grocery purchases.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically 30 days, with expedited processing available in urgent cases
applicationGuide.tip: Don't skip medical expense deductions — seniors can deduct medical costs over $35/month, which often increases benefits significantly.

contentSections.text: Most households now have no asset limit as of March 2024, so your parent can keep their savings and still qualify. Asset limits only apply in special disqualification cases.

faqs.question: What's the difference between MiCAFE and regular SNAP?
faqs.answer: MiCAFE isn't a separate program — it's specialized application help for standard SNAP benefits designed for seniors 60+. Because nearly half of eligible seniors never apply due to stigma or confusion, MiCAFE provides personalized phone support and in-home assistance to help them navigate the process.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and savings if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Her primary home doesn't count toward asset limits, and most households have no asset limit as of March 2024. Asset limits of $3,000-$4,500 only apply in special disqualification cases, which are rare.

faqs.question: What if her Social Security puts her over the income limit?
faqs.answer: Seniors get higher income limits (200% of Federal Poverty Level for gross income) and special deductions for medical expenses over $35/month, plus shelter costs. MiCAFE specialists calculate these deductions — many seniors qualify even when their initial income seems too high.

faqs.question: Where can she use the benefits?
faqs.answer: The Michigan Bridge Card works at grocery stores, supermarkets, and participating farmers markets throughout Michigan. She cannot use it for alcohol, tobacco, or prepared hot foods, but it covers most groceries including fresh produce, meat, dairy, and pantry staples.

faqs.question: What happens if she needs help reapplying next year?
faqs.answer: SNAP benefits require annual recertification. MiCAFE helps with this renewal process too — just call the same number (1-877-664-2233) when recertification time approaches.

faqs.question: How quickly will she start receiving benefits?
faqs.answer: Standard processing takes up to 30 days, but expedited processing is available for urgent situations (like very low income or minimal food resources). Once approved, benefits load automatically onto her Bridge Card each month.

programs.name: Michigan LIHEAP (Energy Assistance)
programs.tagline: Help with heating and electric bills when your parent can't afford to keep up with payments.

localEntities.name: Local MDHHS offices
MI 211 community partners
Utilities like Michigan Gas Utilities, Country Lines
Thaw Fund partners

programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling to pay heating or electric bills, Michigan LIHEAP provides one-time grants to cover past-due amounts, reconnection fees, and energy-related home repairs. The program also helps with deliverable fuels like propane. Because this is crisis-focused assistance, your parent can qualify even if they were previously denied — eligibility expanded in recent years.

Your parent automatically qualifies if they receive SNAP, Medicaid, SSI, or several other programs, regardless of income. Otherwise, household income must be at or below 60% of state median income. Because funding is limited and distributed first-come, first-served, apply as early as possible during heating season.
programs.savingsSource: Variable grant amounts based on need and available funding

otherRequirements: Proof of ID and Social Security Number
Demonstration of energy crisis (past due bill, shut-off notice, or payment plan difficulties)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through MI Bridges or call MI 211 for local assistance — processing times vary by region and funding availability.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect ID, Social Security card, income proof, and utility bills or shut-off notices

steps.title: Apply through MI Bridges online
steps.description: Go to michigan.gov/mibridges to complete the application, or call MI 211 to find local assistance

steps.title: Submit crisis documentation
steps.description: Provide past-due bills, shut-off notices, or payment plan details to demonstrate energy need

steps.title: Follow up with local agency
steps.description: Contact your assigned local MDHHS office or community partner for application status

applicationGuide.waitlist: Limited funds distributed first-come, first-served; potential waitlist or denial if funds exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in heating season (November-March) when most funding becomes available

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (60% State Median)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits; adds approximately $11,236 per additional household member

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Crisis Aid

categories.name: Identity
items: Photo ID (driver's license or state ID)
Social Security card or number verification

categories.name: Financial
items: Award letters for SNAP, Medicaid, SSI if received

contentSections.text: Funding is limited and distributed first-come, first-served. Apply as early as possible during heating season (November-March) when most assistance becomes available.
If your parent was previously denied, reapply. Eligibility requirements changed and no longer require a State Emergency Relief decision letter — many who were denied before now qualify.

faqs.question: What if my parent receives SNAP or Medicaid — do they still need to meet income limits?
faqs.answer: No. If your parent receives SNAP, Medicaid, SSI, Home Heating Credit, or several other programs, they automatically qualify regardless of income. You'll still need to show proof of the energy crisis (past-due bills or shut-off notice).

faqs.question: Can my parent get help if they've already received energy assistance this year?
faqs.answer: It depends on the type of assistance and funding availability. Crisis aid through LIHEAP is typically one-time per heating season, but different programs (state MEAP vs federal LIHEAP) may have separate eligibility periods.

faqs.question: What if my parent's utility company isn't participating in the program?
faqs.answer: Utilities that opt out of LIEAF must provide equivalent assistance. They also cannot shut off service from November 1 to April 15. Contact the utility directly to ask about their assistance programs.

faqs.question: How much assistance can my parent receive?
faqs.answer: Grant amounts vary based on need, available funding, and household size. The program can cover past-due bills, reconnection fees, deliverable fuels like propane, and energy-related home repairs, but exact amounts aren't fixed.

faqs.question: What counts as an 'energy crisis' for eligibility?
faqs.answer: A past-due utility bill, shut-off notice, difficulty keeping up with a payment plan, or need for deliverable fuel or energy-related home repairs. You'll need documentation like bills or notices to prove the crisis.

faqs.question: Are there extra protections for seniors?
faqs.answer: Yes. If your parent is 65+, some utilities offer Winter Protection Plans with additional shut-off protections and may not require payment during certain months. Contact their utility company directly to ask about senior-specific programs.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.tagline: If your parent's home has high energy bills, they may qualify for free insulation, weatherproofing, and heating system repairs to reduce monthly costs.

localEntities.name: MDHHS contracts with local agencies like Kent County (616-632-7950), Wayne Metro, NEMCSA, Community Action Allegan, OLHSA.

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with high heating bills or lives in a drafty home, Michigan's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free energy improvements that can significantly reduce monthly utility costs. The program sends certified technicians to perform a complete energy audit, then installs insulation, weatherstripping, programmable thermostats, and can even replace old furnaces or water heaters — all at no cost.

Your parent qualifies if their household income falls below roughly $2,500/month for one person (varies slightly by region), and they own their home or have landlord permission to rent. Because this program addresses the root causes of high energy bills rather than providing temporary assistance, the improvements can save hundreds of dollars annually for years to come.

Funding is limited and the process takes several months from application to completion, so apply early in the year when new funding becomes available.

summary: Income below 200% of federal poverty level
Own home or have landlord permission
Home not weatherized in last 15 years
otherRequirements: Home must be occupied by applicant
Home cannot be for sale or in foreclosure
Must provide access to basement and attic
Home cannot have major structural issues like damaged roof, mold, or faulty wiring

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Community Action Agency to start the screening process — expect several months from application to completion.

steps.title: Find your local agency
steps.description: Michigan contracts with different agencies by county. Find yours using the directory below or call the main MDHHS line.

steps.title: Complete initial screening
steps.description: Provide income documentation and basic home information. Agency will determine if you meet income requirements and if funding is available.

steps.title: Schedule energy audit
steps.description: If pre-qualified, a certified technician visits to assess your home's energy efficiency and identify needed improvements.

steps.title: Review work plan
steps.description: Agency explains what improvements your home qualifies for based on the audit results.

steps.title: Complete installation
steps.description: Certified contractors perform all approved weatherization work at no cost to you.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Screenings depend on available funding; funds limited, apply early
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the calendar year when new federal funding typically becomes available to local agencies.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (Wayne Metro Region)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits. Other regions may vary slightly. Based on gross household income over last month for Wayne Metro area.

contentSections.heading: What the Program Covers vs. Doesn't Cover

tiers.name: Included Services
tiers.description: Energy efficiency and health/safety improvements based on audit
tiers.coverage: Home energy audit, weatherstripping/caulking/air sealing, insulation for attic/foundation/walls, programmable thermostat, furnace/water heater tune-up or replacement, refrigerator replacement, LED lightbulbs, door repair/replacement (some areas)

tiers.name: Excluded Services
tiers.description: Structural repairs and major home renovations
tiers.coverage: Structural repairs, general rehabilitation, plumbing, electrical work, roof repairs, window replacement, door replacement (most areas)

categories.name: Identity
items: Government ID, passport, or Social Security card for all household members 18+
Proof of home ownership or signed landlord permission form

categories.name: Financial
items: Last 3 months of pay stubs for all working household members

categories.name: Home
items: Most recent electric and gas bills
Documentation that home hasn't been weatherized since 2006

contentSections.text: Funding is limited and agencies only accept applications when money is available. The entire process takes several months, so apply early in the year.
This isn't an emergency repair program. Homes with major issues like damaged roofs, mold, asbestos, or faulty electrical wiring won't qualify until those problems are fixed separately.

faqs.question: What if my parent's home has some repair issues — will that disqualify them?
faqs.answer: Major structural problems like a damaged roof, mold, standing water, asbestos, or knob-and-tube wiring will disqualify the home. Minor issues might be addressed as part of the weatherization work, but this program focuses on energy efficiency, not structural repairs.

faqs.question: My parent rents — can they still get weatherization services?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the landlord must sign a permission agreement allowing the work and agreeing not to raise rent or evict tenants because of the improvements. The agency will provide the required forms.

faqs.question: How much will this actually save on monthly bills?
faqs.answer: Savings vary widely based on the home's condition and what improvements are made. The program doesn't guarantee specific dollar savings, but typical weatherization can reduce heating and cooling costs significantly through better insulation and more efficient equipment.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits vary slightly between regions, so check with your local agency for their specific table. People receiving SSI, State Disability Assistance, or Family Independence Program automatically qualify regardless of the income calculation.

faqs.question: Can they apply again if their home was weatherized years ago?
faqs.answer: Homes cannot be weatherized again if they received services within the last 15 years (or since 2006 in some areas). This is a one-time benefit per property.

faqs.question: What happens if the energy audit shows the home doesn't need much work?
faqs.answer: No services are guaranteed — everything depends on the audit results. If the audit shows the home is already energy efficient or the potential savings don't justify the work, no improvements may be made.

programs.name: Michigan Medicare Assistance Program (MMAP)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one Medicare counseling to help navigate your parent's health insurance options.

localEntities.name: Statewide hotline connects to nearest; examples: CareWell Services Southwest, The Senior Alliance, local Area Agencies on Aging

programs.intro:
If Medicare feels overwhelming for your parent, MMAP provides free, unbiased counseling to help navigate their options. Trained counselors explain Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D in plain English, compare Medigap policies, and help resolve billing issues or appeals. Because MMAP counselors cannot sell insurance, their advice is completely neutral — they work only for your parent's best interests.

MMAP also screens for Medicare Savings Programs that could reduce your parent's premiums and co-pays. For example, if your parent's income is below $1,325/month (single) or $1,783/month (married), they may qualify for programs that eliminate Medicare premiums entirely. The service is available to all Michigan residents regardless of age or income — seniors, people with disabilities, and their caregivers can all get help.

otherRequirements: Must live in Michigan

applicationGuide.summary: Call 1-800-803-7174 to connect with your nearest MMAP office for immediate screening and appointment scheduling.

steps.title: Call the statewide hotline
steps.description: Dial 1-800-803-7174. The system connects you to the MMAP office nearest to your parent's location.

steps.title: Schedule counseling session
steps.description: Discuss your parent's needs and schedule a phone or in-person appointment with a trained counselor.

steps.title: Gather documents (optional)
steps.description: Bring Medicare card, recent bills, and insurance statements if you have them, but counseling can start without paperwork.

steps.title: Meet with counselor
steps.description: Get personalized guidance on Medicare options, savings programs, and next steps for enrollment or appeals.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate phone screening; counseling appointments scheduled promptly
applicationGuide.tip: You don't need any paperwork to start — counselors can explain options first, then help gather documents later.

contentSections.heading: Medicare Savings Program Income Limits (MMAP Can Screen For These)

rows.benefit: Pays Medicare Part B premium only
Pays Medicare Part B premium only

contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits; MMAP counselors screen for these programs during appointments

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits for Medicare Savings Programs
excluded: Burial funds up to $1,500 per person

tiers.name: QMB
tiers.coverage: Pays ALL Medicare costs — premiums, deductibles, co-pays

tiers.name: SLMB
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premium only ($174.70/month in 2026)

tiers.name: QI
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premium only ($174.70/month in 2026)

contentSections.heading: What to Bring (Optional for First Visit)

contentSections.text: MMAP counselors cannot sell insurance or enroll your parent in plans — they only provide education and guidance. This means their advice is completely unbiased and focused on your parent's needs, not sales commissions.

faqs.question: Can MMAP help if my parent already has Medicare coverage?
faqs.answer: Yes. MMAP helps with existing Medicare issues like understanding bills, filing appeals, comparing Medigap policies, and finding prescription drug assistance. They also review coverage annually to ensure your parent has the best plan for their needs.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicare Savings Program limits?
faqs.answer: MMAP counselors can still help find other cost-saving programs like Extra Help for prescription drugs, state pharmaceutical assistance programs, or manufacturer discount programs. They know all available options, not just the income-limited programs.

faqs.question: Can I get help as a caregiver even if I don't live in Michigan?
faqs.answer: If your parent lives in Michigan, you can call MMAP for guidance as their caregiver. The counselors can explain your parent's options and help you understand how to assist them with Medicare decisions.

faqs.question: How long does a MMAP counseling session take?
faqs.answer: Sessions typically last 30-60 minutes depending on complexity. Simple questions about Medicare basics take less time, while comprehensive plan comparisons or appeals assistance may require longer sessions or follow-up appointments.

faqs.question: What's the difference between MMAP and insurance agents?
faqs.answer: MMAP counselors cannot sell insurance and receive no commissions, so their advice is completely neutral. Insurance agents can enroll your parent in plans but may have financial incentives to recommend certain options. MMAP provides unbiased education first.

faqs.question: Can MMAP help with Medicare Advantage plan problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. MMAP counselors help with Medicare Advantage issues including understanding coverage denials, filing appeals, switching plans during open enrollment, and comparing Medicare Advantage to Original Medicare plus Medigap.

programs.name: Home Delivered Meals (Meals on Wheels)
programs.tagline: If your parent has trouble cooking or leaving home, they may qualify for daily meal delivery at no cost.

localEntities.name: Local Area Agency on Aging (contact via michigan.gov/mdhhs)
Meals on Wheels Western Michigan (Kent/Allegan Counties): mealsonwheelswesternmichigan.org, (616) 459-3111
Meals on Wheels Southwest Michigan: snsmeals.org
Mom's Meals for Medicaid: momsmeals.com

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to cook meals or can't safely leave home regularly, Home Delivered Meals (commonly known as Meals on Wheels) provides one or more nutritionally balanced meals delivered to their door each day. Because this program prioritizes older adults who are homebound and cannot prepare meals independently, your parent won't need to meet income limits — eligibility focuses on their functional needs instead.

The program serves adults 60 and older who have difficulty shopping, cooking, or leaving home unassisted. Meals are delivered by local providers and may include special diets for conditions like diabetes or heart failure. Your parent can contribute voluntarily toward the cost, but payment is never required.

Because Home Delivered Meals operates through local Area Agencies on Aging rather than a single statewide program, eligibility and wait times vary by region. Some areas may have waitlists due to high demand and limited funding.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound (difficulty leaving home unassisted regularly) and unable to prepare meals or shop independently due to physical, mental, or cognitive limitations. Must have no able or willing adult in home to prepare all meals.
otherRequirements: Must be home for delivery or arrange ahead for absences
Must be able to feed self or have caregiver assistance available
Special dietary needs must be safely met by program meals

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for a home assessment — meals typically start within a few days if eligible.

steps.title: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Find your regional provider through michigan.gov/mdhhs or call a local Meals on Wheels organization directly.

steps.title: Schedule a home assessment
steps.description: A staff member will visit or call to assess your parent's needs and determine eligibility based on their ability to prepare meals and leave home.

steps.title: Complete consent forms
steps.description: Sign annual consent forms and food allergy waivers. Discuss any dietary restrictions or medical conditions.

steps.title: Begin meal delivery
steps.description: Meals typically start within a few days. Your parent will be reassessed every 6 months to ensure continued eligibility.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Within a few days of assessment if eligible
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible due to funding and availability, varies by region
applicationGuide.tip: Prepare to discuss your parent's specific limitations with cooking, shopping, and leaving home — concrete examples help assessors determine eligibility.

offices.name: Local Area Agency on Aging
offices.address: Find your regional office at michigan.gov/mdhhs

categories.name: Basic Information
items: Proof of age (driver's license or birth certificate)

categories.name: Health Information
items: List of dietary restrictions and food allergies
Information about medical conditions requiring special diets
Medicaid enrollment details if applying through a health plan

contentSections.text: This is not a single centralized program. You must contact your local Area Agency on Aging or regional Meals on Wheels provider — eligibility and wait times vary significantly by location.

If your parent is frequently away from home or travels regularly, they may not qualify as homebound. However, the program can extend to spouses of any age and disabled household members if funding is available.

faqs.question: What if my parent can still cook sometimes but struggles with shopping?
faqs.answer: Your parent may still qualify if they cannot shop independently due to physical, mental, or cognitive limitations, even if they can prepare simple meals. The assessment focuses on their overall ability to maintain adequate nutrition independently.

faqs.question: Can my parent's spouse get meals too if they're under 60?
faqs.answer: Yes, spouses of any age may be eligible for meal delivery if funding is available in your area. Disabled household members and caregivers may also qualify depending on local resources.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent isn't home when meals are delivered?
faqs.answer: Your parent must be home for delivery or arrange ahead of time for absences. Regular absences can disqualify them from the program since it's designed for homebound individuals. They should notify the program about planned absences like medical appointments.

faqs.question: How much will my parent be expected to contribute toward meals?
faqs.answer: Contributions are voluntary — your parent is never required to pay. Many participants contribute what they can afford, but inability to pay will not affect eligibility or service.

faqs.question: What if my parent has diabetes or other dietary restrictions?
faqs.answer: Many programs offer medically tailored meals for conditions like diabetes, heart failure, or recent hospitalizations. During the assessment, discuss your parent's specific dietary needs to ensure the program can safely meet their requirements.

faqs.question: How long does my parent have to wait for an assessment?
faqs.answer: Assessment timing varies by local provider and demand. Some areas can schedule within days, while others may have longer waits. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging as soon as possible to get on their schedule.

programs.name: Respite Care (via MI Choice Waiver)
programs.tagline: Get temporary caregiver relief for your parent plus home care services if they're at risk of nursing home placement.

localEntities.name: Local MI Choice Waiver Agencies (find via regional AAAs)
Examples: CareWell Services of Southwest Michigan, The Senior Alliance (734.722.2830), Region 7 Area Agency on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, MI Choice Waiver provides respite care to give you temporary relief from caregiving duties. Because this waiver covers multiple services under one program, your parent also gets adult day care, home-delivered meals, personal emergency response systems, and supports coordination — preventing nursing home placement.

Your parent must be at risk of nursing home placement and pass a clinical assessment called Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC). Income limit: $2,982/month for a single person (2025). Asset limit: $9,950 excluding home and one car. Home equity cannot exceed $730,000 if your parent lives there or intends to return.

This program serves all Michigan counties through 16 regional agencies. Because each agency determines specific respite hours based on your parent's assessed needs, the amount of relief varies by individual situation.

summary: At risk of nursing home placement
Need daily help with basic activities
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or have cognitive/behavioral issues that require supervision (assessed through in-person clinical evaluation)
otherRequirements: Michigan resident
Medicaid eligible
Living in community setting (not nursing home)
Must need supports coordination plus at least one other waiver service

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local MI Choice Agency for telephone intake — processing time varies by region.

steps.title: Find your local MI Choice Agency
steps.description: Michigan has 16 regional agencies. Find yours through the state website or call examples like The Senior Alliance at 734-722-2830 for Wayne County area.

steps.title: Complete telephone intake
steps.description: Agency specialists conduct initial screening over the phone using MI Choice Waiver Referral Form information.

steps.title: Schedule in-home assessment
steps.description: MI Choice Agency schedules Level of Care Determination (LOCD) visit to assess your parent's functional needs in person.

steps.title: Await eligibility determination
steps.description: Agency determines if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care plus financial eligibility requirements.

applicationGuide.tip: Gather all financial documents before the phone intake — income and asset verification speeds up the process.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home
excluded: Life insurance with face value under $1,500

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing functional limitations
Documentation of help needed with daily activities

contentSections.text: Your parent must need supports coordination PLUS at least one other service to qualify. A dementia diagnosis alone does not guarantee eligibility — they must also need help with daily activities like bathing or dressing.

If your parents are married, special spousal impoverishment protections may allow higher asset and income limits for the community spouse. Ask your MI Choice Agency about these protections during intake.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for MI Choice?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as home equity doesn't exceed $730,000 and she lives there or intends to return. The home doesn't count toward the $9,950 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over $2,982 per month?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify. The income limit is firm at 300% of the SSI federal benefit rate. However, if he's married, special spousal rules may apply that could help with eligibility.

faqs.question: How many hours of respite care will we get?
faqs.answer: Hours vary based on your parent's assessed needs and the agency's available resources. The MI Choice Agency determines this during the in-home evaluation — there's no fixed statewide amount.

faqs.question: Can Dad get MI Choice if he's already in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, if he lives in adult foster care or a home for the aged. He cannot get MI Choice if he's in a nursing home, but other community settings qualify.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom needs nursing home care later?
faqs.answer: She can transition off MI Choice Waiver to regular Medicaid nursing home coverage. The waiver is designed to delay or prevent nursing home placement, not replace it when medically necessary.

faqs.question: Do we have to pay anything out of pocket?
faqs.answer: No, MI Choice Waiver services are free if your parent qualifies. However, they may have a small Medicaid copay for some services based on income.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 55+ and unemployed, they can earn while training for new employment — 20 hours per week at minimum wage.

localEntities.name: The Senior Alliance (serves specific service area; contact Lauren Wonsowski)
MSC-MW (serves Macomb, Oakland, St. Clair Counties)
Area Agency on Aging (serves Berrien, Cass, Van Buren Counties)
CWI Works (operates through ~50 local partner organizations)
NEMCSA (serves specific regions)
Contact Michigan Department of Health and Human Services, Bureau of Aging, Community Living, and Support for complete provider list

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55 or older and struggling to find work, SCSEP provides paid job training while they search for permanent employment. Your parent earns minimum wage for 20 hours per week while getting hands-on work experience, computer training, and professional job placement help.

Because SCSEP combines income with training, your parent builds their resume and develops new skills instead of just receiving benefits. The program is designed as a bridge to unsubsidized employment — participants work in community organizations while preparing for permanent jobs in the private sector.

Income limits are strict: $19,562 annually for a single person, $26,437 for a couple (2026). Your parent must be completely unemployed and willing to commit to both training hours and active job searching.
programs.savingsSource: 20 hours per week at federal minimum wage ($7.25/hour) equals approximately $3,770 annually; higher state minimum wages increase earnings

otherRequirements: Must be unemployed (not underemployed or part-time employed)
Must register with Michigan Works
Must commit to 19-20 hours per week training
Must participate in job search activities
Must provide proof of all household income and family size

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local SCSEP provider by phone or visit a Michigan Works office — processing times vary by region.

steps.title: Register with Michigan Works
steps.description: This is required before applying to SCSEP. Find your local office at michigan.gov/michiganworks.

steps.title: Find your regional SCSEP provider
steps.description: Michigan has multiple providers serving different counties. Contact Michigan DHHS at 517-241-4598 for your area's provider.

steps.title: Gather income documentation
steps.description: You'll need proof of all household income, family size, age verification, and proof of unemployment status.

steps.title: Submit application to local provider
steps.description: Each regional provider has their own application process — some accept online, others require in-person visits.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority given to veterans, people over 65, those with disabilities, and rural residents — others may wait longer
applicationGuide.tip: Call 517-241-4598 first to identify your regional provider — this varies by county and will save time.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (125% Federal Poverty Level)

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of all household income (pay stubs, tax returns, benefit statements)

categories.name: Employment
items: Employment history or resume if available

contentSections.text: Veterans and people over 65 get priority enrollment. If your parent doesn't fall into a priority category, they may face a waitlist depending on local demand.
Your parent must be completely unemployed to qualify — being underemployed or working part-time disqualifies them from SCSEP.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and regular unemployment benefits?
faqs.answer: SCSEP provides paid work experience and training, not just income support. Your parent works 20 hours per week at a community organization while getting job placement help. Because they're earning wages, not receiving unemployment benefits, they're building skills and work history.

faqs.question: Can Mom do this if she's getting Social Security?
faqs.answer: Social Security income counts toward the $19,562 annual limit for single people. If her Social Security plus any other household income exceeds this threshold, she won't qualify for SCSEP.

faqs.question: How long can Dad stay in the program?
faqs.answer: SCSEP is designed as temporary training to help participants find unsubsidized employment. Specific time limits aren't provided in available information — ask your local provider about maximum participation periods.

faqs.question: What if we live in a rural area?
faqs.answer: Over 60% of SCSEP providers serve rural regions, and rural residents get priority enrollment. Contact the Michigan DHHS Bureau of Aging at 517-241-4598 to find providers serving your area.

faqs.question: Does this affect other benefits like Medicaid or SNAP?
faqs.answer: SCSEP wages count as income for other means-tested programs. If your parent receives SNAP or Medicaid, notify those programs about SCSEP earnings to avoid overpayments or benefit issues.

faqs.question: What if Mom has been unemployed for years?
faqs.answer: Long-term unemployment doesn't disqualify her. SCSEP specifically serves people with 'low employment prospects,' which can include those who've been out of work for extended periods. The program provides updated training and work experience to help with reentry.

programs.name: Legal Assistance for Seniors
programs.tagline: Free legal help for your parent on housing, benefits, family issues, and elder rights if they're 60+ in Michigan.

localEntities.name: Legal Aid of Western Michigan (lawestmi.org)
Legal Services of Eastern Michigan (lsem-mi.org)
Lakeshore Legal Aid (lakeshorelegalaid.org)
LSNM (lsnm.org)
State Bar of Michigan Modest Means (lrs.michbar.org)

programs.intro:
If your parent is facing legal problems with housing, government benefits, family issues, or discrimination, they may qualify for free legal services in Michigan. Because these are regional nonprofit organizations, seniors 60+ often qualify regardless of income, though low-income families get priority.

These services cover civil legal matters only — housing disputes, benefit denials, elder abuse, foreclosure prevention, discrimination cases, and family law issues. Legal aid attorneys can provide advice, represent your parent in court, or advocate on their behalf at no charge. Criminal defense is not included.

Eligibility varies by region because different nonprofit organizations serve different parts of Michigan. Most prioritize households with income at or below 125% of the Federal Poverty Level, but many waive income requirements entirely for seniors 60+.

otherRequirements: Michigan resident
Civil legal matters only
Cannot be incarcerated

applicationGuide.summary: Call 888-783-8190 or apply online at MichiganLegalAid.org — intake staff will assess eligibility before providing services.

steps.title: Find your regional provider
steps.description: Visit MichiganLegalAid.org or call 888-783-8190 to be connected to the legal aid organization serving your parent's area.

steps.title: Complete intake assessment
steps.description: Provide information about your parent's income, household size, assets, and the specific legal issue. Have bank statements and identification ready.

steps.title: Get matched with services
steps.description: If eligible, your parent will be connected with a legal aid attorney or referred to appropriate resources based on their specific legal needs.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Depends on lawyer availability in your region
applicationGuide.tip: Seniors 60+ often qualify regardless of income, so apply even if your parent's income seems too high.

contentSections.text: This is not one statewide program — you must contact the specific legal aid organization serving your parent's area for exact eligibility requirements and available services.
Asset limits vary by region. In western Michigan, your parent cannot have more than $15,000 in countable assets, but their home, car, and retirement accounts don't count toward this limit.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Many providers waive income requirements entirely for seniors 60+. Even if income matters in your region, apply anyway — eligibility is assessed case-by-case and seniors often get priority.

faqs.question: Will Mom need to pay anything upfront?
faqs.answer: No. Legal aid services are completely free if your parent qualifies. There's also a Modest Means Program through the State Bar that charges reduced fees ($75/hour after a $25 consultation) if she doesn't qualify for free services.

faqs.question: Can they help with Dad's criminal case?
faqs.answer: No. Legal aid only handles civil matters like housing disputes, benefit denials, elder abuse, family law, and discrimination. Criminal defense is not included.

faqs.question: What if we can't find a legal aid office in our area?
faqs.answer: Call 888-783-8190 or visit MichiganLegalAid.org — they'll connect you to the right regional provider or refer you to other resources if legal aid doesn't serve your specific area.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get help?
faqs.answer: Eligibility is assessed during the initial intake call or application. Getting connected with an attorney depends on availability and case complexity in your region — there may be a waitlist for non-urgent matters.

faqs.question: Will this affect Mom's other benefits?
faqs.answer: No. Getting legal aid services won't impact your parent's Social Security, Medicare, SNAP, or other government benefits. These services are designed to help protect and access benefits, not reduce them.

programs.name: Nursing Home Long-Term Care Ombudsman
programs.tagline: Free advocate when your parent has problems with their nursing home or assisted living facility care.

localEntities.name: MEJI (state ombudsman staff)
Area Agencies on Aging statewide
AgeWays (specific counties)

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a nursing home, assisted living, or adult foster care facility in Michigan and has concerns about their care, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman provides free advocacy to resolve problems. Because ombudsmen are independent advocates (not facility employees), they can investigate complaints, explain your parent's rights, and work directly with facilities to address issues like medication errors, poor food quality, or staff treatment.

The program serves all residents in licensed long-term care facilities statewide, regardless of income. Your parent's consent is required — ombudsmen work at the direction of residents, not families, to protect resident autonomy and confidentiality.

summary: Resident of licensed long-term care facility
otherRequirements: Must be resident of licensed nursing home, assisted living, adult foster care, or home for the aged in Michigan
Services provided only with resident's consent

applicationGuide.summary: Call 866-485-9393 to reach your local ombudsman — response is immediate for urgent concerns.

steps.title: Call the toll-free number
steps.description: Dial 866-485-9393 to connect with your local ombudsman based on your parent's facility location.

steps.title: Describe the concern
steps.description: Explain the issue with care, services, or treatment. No paperwork required initially.

steps.title: Get your parent's permission
steps.description: The ombudsman will need your parent's consent to investigate or take action on their behalf.

steps.title: Work with the advocate
steps.description: The ombudsman investigates, mediates with facility staff, and follows up until the issue is resolved.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate response for concerns; ombudsmen provide timely assistance upon contact
applicationGuide.tip: If it's an emergency involving immediate health or safety, also call 911 or the facility administrator directly.

contentSections.text: Your parent must give permission for the ombudsman to act. Families cannot file complaints without the resident's consent, even if the resident has dementia — this protects resident rights and autonomy.

faqs.question: Can I call the ombudsman about my mom's care if she has dementia and can't consent?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman will assess your mom's ability to understand and consent to services. If she cannot provide informed consent due to dementia, the ombudsman may still be able to provide information and guidance, but cannot take direct action without proper legal authority or guardianship documentation.

faqs.question: What kinds of problems can the ombudsman help resolve?
faqs.answer: Common issues include medication errors, poor food quality, staff treatment concerns, billing disputes, roommate conflicts, facility policies that restrict rights, and problems with personal belongings. They also help with discharge planning disputes and ensure residents understand their rights.

faqs.question: Will the facility retaliate against my parent if we file a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal and ombudsmen monitor for it specifically. Because ombudsmen maintain confidentiality and work as mediators rather than enforcers, they focus on resolving issues cooperatively while protecting your parent from any negative consequences.

faqs.question: How is this different from calling the state health department about facility problems?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman focuses on advocacy and resolution for individual residents, while the health department conducts regulatory inspections and enforcement. The ombudsman works confidentially at your parent's direction; health department complaints become part of the facility's public record.

faqs.question: What if the ombudsman can't resolve our problem?
faqs.answer: If mediation doesn't work, the ombudsman can refer you to other agencies like Adult Protective Services, the health department, or legal aid. They'll also document patterns of problems for systemic advocacy to improve conditions for all residents.

faqs.question: Does this cost anything or affect my parent's benefits?
faqs.answer: The service is completely free and confidential. Using ombudsman services will not affect Medicaid, Medicare, or any other benefits your parent receives.

programs.name: MI Choice Waiver Program
programs.tagline: Home care services that keep your parent out of a nursing home — if they qualify medically and financially.

localEntities.name: The Senior Alliance (Region 1C, Wayne County): 5454 Venoy Road, Wayne, Michigan 48184-1755
CareWell Services of Southwest Michigan (8-county region)
Region 7 Area Agency on Aging: 1-800-858-1637
AgeWays (serves specific regions)
Contact Michigan Department of Health and Human Services (MDHHS) for your county's specific agency

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay at home, MI Choice Waiver can provide nursing services, respite care, medical equipment, and care coordination at no cost. Because this program covers services that would otherwise cost thousands monthly in private pay, your parent can remain in their own home while receiving professional medical support.

Your parent must be 65+ (or 18+ with disability), have monthly income below $2,982, and pass a rigorous functional assessment proving they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management. The program requires Medicaid eligibility and cannot be used while living in a nursing home — it's specifically designed as a community-based alternative.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require Nursing Facility Level of Care — demonstrated need for daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar care through clinical assessment
otherRequirements: Must qualify for Medicaid
Cannot currently live in nursing home
Must need care coordination plus at least one additional service
Can live in own home, apartment, adult foster care, or home for the aged

applicationGuide.summary: Call your county's MI Choice Waiver Agency for a 30-45 minute phone screening, followed by an in-home assessment by a registered nurse.

steps.title: Find your county's waiver agency
steps.description: Contact Michigan Department of Health and Human Services at (734) 722-2830 to get your county's specific MI Choice Waiver Agency phone number.

steps.title: Complete phone screening
steps.description: Your county agency will conduct a 30-45 minute interview covering your parent's daily living needs, income, and assets.

steps.title: Schedule in-home assessment
steps.description: A registered nurse will visit your parent at home, hospital, or nursing home to complete the Michigan Medicaid Nursing Facility Level of Care Determination (LOCD).

steps.title: Provide financial documentation
steps.description: Submit detailed income and asset statements, plus medical information to verify functional needs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Initial screening: 30-45 minutes; full processing timeline varies by county
applicationGuide.tip: Having a diagnosis like Alzheimer's doesn't automatically qualify your parent — they must demonstrate specific functional limitations through the assessment.

contentSections.heading: 2026 Income Limits
contentSections.footnote: 2026 federal limits. Even $1 over disqualifies applicants.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Life insurance with cash value over $1,500
excluded: Life insurance with no cash value

offices.name: The Senior Alliance
offices.address: 5454 Venoy Road, Wayne, MI 48184 (serves Wayne County except certain cities)

offices.name: CareWell Services
offices.address: Serves Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, St. Joseph, Van Buren counties

offices.name: General Information
offices.address: Call to find your county's specific waiver agency

categories.name: Financial Records
items: Asset statements for investments or property
Power of Attorney documentation if applicable

categories.name: Medical/Functional Information
items: Medical history relevant to daily care needs
Documentation of help needed with bathing, dressing, mobility

contentSections.text: The income limit is strict: $2,982/month for 2026. Even $1 over this amount disqualifies your parent entirely, with no exceptions.
Your parent must need at least two services: care coordination plus one additional service like nursing care or respite. Care coordination alone doesn't qualify.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for MI Choice?
faqs.answer: Yes, she can keep her house as long as the home equity doesn't exceed $730,000 (2025 figure). She must either live in the home or have 'Intent to Return' to it. Home equity is calculated as current value minus outstanding debt.

faqs.question: What if Dad has dementia but can still dress himself most days?
faqs.answer: Having dementia doesn't automatically qualify him. The registered nurse assessment looks at his actual daily functioning — if he can consistently manage dressing, bathing, and other activities without help, he may not meet the Nursing Facility Level of Care requirement.

faqs.question: Can she get MI Choice services while living in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, she can receive MI Choice services while living in adult foster care or home for the aged. However, she cannot use the program while living in a nursing home — it's specifically for community-based care.

faqs.question: What happens if her Social Security puts her $50 over the income limit?
faqs.answer: She won't qualify, period. The $2,982 monthly limit is strict federal law with no exceptions. Even $1 over disqualifies applicants entirely.

faqs.question: How long does the approval process take?
faqs.answer: The initial phone screening takes 30-45 minutes, followed by an in-home nurse assessment. Total processing time varies by county since different regional agencies handle applications. Call your county's specific waiver agency for current timelines.

faqs.question: If she qualifies, what services will she actually receive?
faqs.answer: Services are tailored to her specific needs but can include nursing visits, respite care for family caregivers, medical equipment, personal emergency response systems, and care coordination. The exact mix depends on her assessment and what she needs to safely remain at home.

programs.name: Home Help Program
programs.tagline: Michigan pays family or friends to provide personal care for your parent at home.

localEntities.name: Local MDHHS county offices
Area Agencies on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or getting around but wants to stay home, Michigan's Home Help Program can pay a family member or friend to provide that care. The program covers up to 180 hours per month of personal care services — potentially more based on your parent's assessed needs.

Because this program works through Medicaid, your parent must qualify for Michigan Medicaid first and meet strict income limits ($1,305/month for a single person in 2025). The state will assess your parent's functional needs and can pay caregivers who aren't spouses or parents of minor children.

Unlike nursing home care, this keeps your parent in their own home while ensuring they get professional oversight of their care needs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need hands-on help with at least one daily activity like eating, bathing, dressing, grooming, moving around, or using the toilet (assessed via state form MDHHS-5534)
otherRequirements: Michigan resident
U.S. citizen or legal resident
Living in own home or planning to return

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local MDHHS county office to start the Medicaid application and functional assessment process.

steps.title: Apply for Michigan Medicaid
steps.description: Visit your local MDHHS county office or call to start the Medicaid application. You cannot get Home Help services without Medicaid enrollment first.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: MDHHS will schedule an assessment using form MDHHS-5534 to determine what daily activities your parent needs help with.

steps.title: Identify potential caregivers
steps.description: Choose family members, friends, or neighbors who are 18+ and not your parent's spouse. They'll need background checks and training through the CHAMPS system.

steps.title: Enroll approved caregivers
steps.description: Once approved, caregivers complete CHAMPS enrollment and training to begin receiving payment for care services.

applicationGuide.tip: Start with Medicaid enrollment first — this is the biggest hurdle and everything else depends on it.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2025)
contentSections.footnote: Limits update each April based on Federal Poverty Level

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary residence if your parent, spouse, minor child, or disabled child lives there
Home equity under $752,000 if planning to return home

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of citizenship or legal residency
Utility bill or lease showing Michigan address

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or Social Security award letters
Property deeds or home equity documentation

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care assistance

categories.name: For Caregivers
items: Proof of separate address if not living with your parent

contentSections.text: Your parent must qualify for Medicaid first — this program isn't available through Medicare alone. The income limits are much stricter than other senior programs.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to take care of my own parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you're 18+ and not your parent's spouse. You'll need to pass a background check, complete training through CHAMPS, and be enrolled as an official caregiver. Spouses cannot be paid caregivers under this program.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the $1,305 limit?
faqs.answer: She won't qualify for this program. The income limits are strict and based on 100% of Federal Poverty Level. Consider other options like private pay home care or veterans benefits if applicable.

faqs.question: How many hours of care can we get per month?
faqs.answer: Typically up to 180 hours per month, but additional hours are possible based on your parent's assessed needs and MDHHS approval. The exact amount depends on the functional assessment results.

faqs.question: Does Mom have to sell her house to qualify?
faqs.answer: No, as long as she lives there or plans to return home, and the home equity is under $752,000 (2026). The house is exempt from asset limits in these situations.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: This program provides specific in-home personal care services through Medicaid. Your parent needs regular Medicaid first, then qualifies for these additional home care services based on functional need assessment.

faqs.question: Can we choose any caregiver we want?
faqs.answer: You can choose family, friends, or neighbors who are 18+, but they cannot be your parent's spouse or the parent of a minor child. All caregivers must pass background checks and complete required training.

programs.name: MiCAFE (Michigan's Coordinated Access to Food for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: Free help applying for food stamps, Medicare savings, and utility assistance for your parent age 60+.

localEntities.name: Elder Law of Michigan, Inc. (program administrator)
MiCAFE Network partners statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, they may be missing out on hundreds of dollars in monthly benefits. Nearly 50% of eligible seniors don't enroll in SNAP (food stamps) without help navigating the application process.

MiCAFE provides free, one-on-one assistance to help your parent apply for SNAP benefits (Bridge Card), Medicare premium help, prescription drug assistance, and utility programs. Because MiCAFE handles the paperwork and follows up on applications, your parent gets personalized support without the confusion of government forms.

The program serves all of Michigan through Elder Law of Michigan and local partner organizations. In-home assistance may be available in your area.

summary: No asset or income requirements for navigation help
otherRequirements: Living on limited income
Michigan resident

applicationGuide.summary: Call MiCAFE directly — they'll assess what programs your parent qualifies for and handle the applications.

steps.title: Call MiCAFE intake line
steps.description: Call 1-877-664-2233 (Monday-Friday, 9:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m.) to discuss your parent's situation.

steps.title: Complete eligibility screening
steps.description: MiCAFE staff will review income, assets, and medical expenses to determine which programs your parent qualifies for.

steps.title: Sign authorization form
steps.description: Complete authorization allowing MiCAFE to submit applications on your parent's behalf.

steps.title: MiCAFE submits applications
steps.description: Staff electronically submit applications for SNAP, Medicare savings, utility assistance, and other eligible programs.

steps.title: Annual recertification support
steps.description: MiCAFE will contact your parent when benefits need renewal and help with recertification paperwork.

applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's Social Security number, monthly income, and recent medical bills ready when you call.

contentSections.text: MiCAFE doesn't provide benefits directly — they help your parent apply for existing programs like SNAP, Medicare savings, and utility assistance. Even if your parent doesn't qualify for food stamps, they may be eligible for other programs MiCAFE can help with.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for SNAP but they still need help with other expenses?
faqs.answer: MiCAFE can still help your parent apply for Medicare premium assistance, prescription drug programs, and utility assistance. These programs have different income limits than SNAP, so your parent may qualify even if they don't qualify for food benefits.

faqs.question: Does MiCAFE cost anything?
faqs.answer: No. MiCAFE navigation services are completely free. They're funded to help seniors access benefits they're already entitled to.

faqs.question: Can MiCAFE help if my parent lives in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes. MiCAFE can help residents of assisted living facilities apply for programs they qualify for, including SNAP benefits that can reduce their out-of-pocket food costs.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's application gets denied?
faqs.answer: MiCAFE staff can help your parent understand why the application was denied and assist with appeals if appropriate. They can also help identify other programs your parent might qualify for.

faqs.question: How often does my parent need to recertify for benefits?
faqs.answer: Most programs require annual recertification. MiCAFE will contact your parent before benefits expire and help complete renewal paperwork to avoid interruptions.

faqs.question: Can I call MiCAFE on behalf of my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can call to get information and start the process. Your parent will need to provide authorization for MiCAFE to submit applications and communicate with government agencies on their behalf.
